title: How the Germans left Florence in August 1944 | get back, lauretta!
highlights:
How the Germans left Florence in August 1944 | get back, lauretta!

Firenze, 3 agosto 1944 All the bridges in Florence over the Arno, except for the Ponte Vecchio, were destroyed by Germans as the Allied Forces took Florence. While they didn’t destroy the Ponte Vecchio, they bombed the north and south ends of the bridge, destroying everything. Un ufficiale Inglese osserva i danni sul Ponte Vecchio. title: The Florence Summer of ‘44
highlights: The summer of 2014 marks the 70th anniversary of Tuscany’s liberation from the Nazi forces.  ...    The year was 1944 and World War II was winding down in Europe. The Germans occupied Florence and the Allies were advancing on the city from Rome. To slow down the Allies, who intended to march straight through the city to the German lines, the bridges, bell towers and center of Florence were mined with bombs and explosives. The German decision violated all prior agreements that designated it as an “open city” (città aperta). Both sides had  ...    The drama started on July 29, when a notice was posted ordering all inhabitants along the river to evacuate before noon the following day. Young children, the elderly, men and women all carried whatever possessions they could manage by hand. Some went to relatives in other parts of the city, but many took refuge in the enormous Pitti Palace.
